question: Which event catches unhandled promise rejections?

options.text: unhandledrejection
error
options.explanation: The error event catches synchronous errors and resource loading errors, but not promise rejections

options.text: rejectionhandled
options.explanation: rejectionhandled fires when a previously unhandled rejection gets a handler — it's the reverse event

options.text: promiseerror
options.explanation: No such event exists in JavaScript

question: Why should a logger never throw errors?

options.text: The global handler will catch the logger's error and call the logger again — infinite loop
JavaScript forbids throw inside network requests
options.explanation: JavaScript doesn't forbid throw in network requests — the problem is an architectural cycle

options.text: A logger error will automatically terminate the process
options.explanation: The process won't necessarily terminate, but there will be an infinite loop or stack overflow

options.text: The logger runs in a separate thread and can't throw errors
options.explanation: The logger runs in the main JavaScript thread, like all other code

question: Why is a global error handler a safety net, not the primary strategy?

options.text: The global handler doesn't know the error context
It can't show the error next to the relevant UI element
It can't offer retry or rollback of the operation
The global handler is slower than local try/catch
options.explanation: Speed is not the issue — it's about context and ability to respond

question: Why is Date.now() a poor choice for a unique error ID?

options.text: Two calls in the same millisecond will produce the same ID
Date.now() returns a string but a number is needed
options.explanation: Date.now() returns a number — the problem is potential collisions, not the type

options.text: Date.now() only works in Node.js
options.explanation: Date.now() works in both browser and Node.js

options.text: Date.now() is not defined in TypeScript
options.explanation: Date.now() is fully supported in TypeScript

question: What happens if you subscribe to the error event but forget about unhandledrejection?

options.text: Unhandled promise rejections will remain completely invisible to monitoring
The error event will automatically catch promises too
options.explanation: The error event doesn't catch promise rejections — a separate handler is needed

options.text: JavaScript will show a warning about the missing handler
options.explanation: The browser will show a console error, but your monitoring won't know about it

options.text: All promises will be automatically resolved
options.explanation: The absence of a handler doesn't affect promise execution — they still reject
